title: [release-v4.3] compat,build: handle docker's preconfigured `cacheTo`,`cacheFrom`
body:
Backport of https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/16380

Docker's newer clients popuates `cacheFrom` and `cacheTo` parameter by default as empty array for all commands but buildah's design of distributed cache expects this to be a repo not image hence parse only the first populated repo and ignore if empty array.

Note: Upstream will start using newer API as soon as buildah exposes new API

```release-note
compat,build: make compat build api functional for newer docker-clients
```

Tests failing
Uh-oh not sure how failing test are related to this PR but checking.

> Tests failing

@mheon Pushed a commit to retrofit test.
Conflict alert. @lsm5 just fixed that in #16519, please rebase to get a proper fix.
/lgtm
Is there a release 4.3.2 scheduled which will contain this fix ?

> Is there a release 4.3.2 scheduled which will contain this fix ?

I think @mheon could confirm this.
No present plans for a release prior to 4.4.0 in late January, but we might do a 4.3.2 later this month if enough bugfixes go in.

I see that yesterday you created a 4.3 branch for RHEL. I know already that if release 4.3 of podman reaches RHEL one day, without this fix, it will break many Amadeus builds. So as soon as this scenario never happens, I am fine.
You'll probably want to make a BZ for a RHEL backport, then - I based v4.3.1-rhel off the preexisting v4.3.1 release, so if this isn't in 4.3.1, it isn't in there.

That's what I was thinking as well. However I still don't understand why we need to report problems upstream on GitHub here, and also need to report problems on Red Hat side. Why do we need to ask explictly as Red Hat clients that Red Hat includes fixes that we reported already upsteam, and which Red Hat fixed already ? For me it hardly makes sense.

@Romain-Geissler-1A When issues are patched upstream in the main branch, as this fix was with #16380, they are incorporated into the next version of Podman that's delivered with the next release of RHEL.  For this issue, that means this fix will appear in Podman v4.4, which is slated for delivery with RHEL 8.8 and RHEL 9.2.  We're delivering the Podman bits into RHEL in late January or early February of 2023, and RHEL  8.8 and RHEL 9.2 will be made available in late April or Early May of 2023 after they are fully tested and packaged.

If that works for you, then you don't need to report this issue in a Bugzilla.

However, if you need Red Hat to backport this or any other fix into an already-released Podman on RHEL, the folks that work for Red Hat can't do that backport for RHEL without a Bugzilla being filed.  

HTH

Hi,

In January/February I expect podman 4.3 to hit RHEL 8.7/9.1 right ? If yes, then I know in advance it will break us. I do acknowledge (still without really understanding why this is needed on top of the GitHub activity) that you really need a Bugzilla for the backport in the future RHEL's 4.3 version. I have opened already a Red Hat ticket couple of days ago, it's the Red Hat support case 03387104 (from which I expect a Bugzilla request will be created by your support teams).

Note: I am a maintainer myself, facing similar requests from time to time.

Cheers,
Romain

@Romain-Geissler-1A, there are no plans at this time to include Podman v4.3 in any RHEL products.  RHEL 8.7 and 9.1 will have Podman v4.2 in them.  Podman v4.4 is planned for RHEL 8.8 and 9.2 which we have to code freeze for in late February 2023, with a RHEL 8.8/9.2 becoming available in May of 2023.

Thanks for opening up the BZ, we will plan to backport this fix to our Podman v4.2 branch and then deliver this with RHEL 8.7.0.2 and 9.1.0.2.  That deadline is mid-January (I think), with a release later in February.  But don't quote me on those dates.

Ah so there is a change in your releasing policy ? I was told (when asking for the exact release date of podman 4.1 for RHEL) that you try to release podman every 12 weeks (and I assumed this was true both for upstream release and RHEL releases as well). So now you release every 12 weeks upstream and you follow the RHEL release cadence every 6 months ? And if this is the case, why was a branch "v4.3.1-rhel" created recently, it's only aimed at targetting CentOS ?

Note: I am just asking for curiosity, like you did here, every Red Hat employee always stress the fact that any announced date is for information only, not a guarantee at all ;) I am fine with any change.
Also, if podman 4.3 will never hit any RHEL, then we can close my support case 03387104 immediately, there is no point backporting this to podman 4.2 as podman 4.2 is not affected.

We release the current Podman release to RHEL every 12 weeks.  Nothing has changed there.

Podman 4.3.1 should be release or will be released soon. podman 4.4 will be released in January/February, 12 weeks or earlier after it is released it will show up in a RHEL release.
@Romain-Geissler-1A @rhatdan @TomSweeneyRedHat Backport PR for `v4.3.1-rhel` https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/16822
